News Highlights: Top Global Markets News of the Day - Wednesday at 7 PM ET
data.description:
Trump Pauses 'Reciprocal' Tariffs, but Hits China Harder

The 10% global baseline levy for all imports will stay in effect.

Nasdaq Soars to Best Day Since 2001 After Trump Pauses Some Tariffs

Stocks surged in a historic rally. The Nasdaq's 12% rise was its biggest move upward since Jan. 3, 2001.

Apple Stock Jumps on Tariff News, Hope for Exemption

President Donald Trump is raising China's tariff rate to 125% but also said he is considering excluding imports by some companies.

Trump's Tariff Pause 'Welcome Reprieve' For Global Economy, Canada PM Carney Says

Carney said the 90-day pause, and Trump's pledge to negotiate new trading arrangements with dozens of countries, "will likely result in a fundamental restructuring of the global trading system."

Minneapolis Fed's Kashkari Says Tariffs Raise the Bar for Fed Interest-Rate Moves

The central bank's "first priority must be keeping long-run inflation expectations anchored," Kashkari says.

Fed Officials Flagged Risks of 'More Persistent' Inflation From Tariffs

Some policymakers at last month's meeting warned of difficult trade-offs that loom if inflation rises and hiring deteriorates.

Bond Market Calms as 10-Year Treasury Auction Sees 'Very Strong' Demand

Worries of a so-called busted sale had been rising.

China Has Readied a Trade-War Arsenal That Takes Aim at U.S. Companies

Beijing's strategy to hit back at President Trump goes well beyond tariffs, targeting companies who bank on their China ties.

EU Member States Approve Response to U.S. Steel Tariffs

European Union members approved an initial list of U.S. goods that will be subject to tariffs, giving the bloc power to impose levies on some products as early as next week.

ECB Has Many Instruments It Can Deploy, Cipollone Says

The European Central Bank has many instruments it can deploy in the event that economic disruption and sharp falls in financial markets threaten to turn into a crisis, a senior official said Wednesday.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Company News of the Day - Wednesday at 7 PM ET
data.description:
Constellation Brands to Divest Cheaper Wine Brands Amid Organizational Review

Alcohol heavyweight Constellation Brands will undergo a review of its organizational structuring that is expected to save the company more than $200 million by 2028.

Walmart Confident About Tariff Resilience Despite Sales Volatility

Walmart reaffirmed its first-quarter sales guidance and widened its range for operating income growth to stay flexible on pricing as tariffs go into effect.

Delta Air Lines Pulls Guidance on Trump Tariff Uncertainty. Why Airline Stocks Are Rising.

Delta Air Lines scraps its full-year guidance Wednesday and warns 'growth has largely stalled' amid global economic uncertainty.

Remy Cointreau CEO to Leave Group

European drinks makers face waning demand after a boom in beverage consumption during the pandemic as well as uncertainty in key markets.

Harley-Davidson Director Resigns Citing 'Grave Concerns' About Company

Jared Dourdeville of the New York investment firm H Partners expressed numerous frustrations, and said the company had suffered from "cultural depletion."

WeightWatchers Prepares for Bankruptcy

The health and wellness company is preparing to file for bankruptcy in the coming months as part of a plan to hand control of the business to its creditors.

Pharma Stocks Slide Further After Trump Reiterates Tariff Threats on Drugs

Drugmakers have said they're still unclear about whether Trump will impose tariffs on finished products or on active pharmaceutical ingredients.

Volkswagen Vehicle Deliveries Rise Despite China Weakness

The automaker has been hit by intense competition in China that has seen rivals cut prices to win customers in its largest market.

Prada Deal for Versace at Risk of Collapsing With Market in Turmoil

Prada's tentative deal to acquire Versace from Capri for roughly $1.4 billion could be announced as soon as Thursday if Prada's namesake family sign off.

Formula One's TV Rights Are Up for Grabs, but Media Companies Aren't Racing to Bid

Liberty Media's F1 is seeking $150 million to $180 million a year for the fast-growing sport.

data.title: Macmahon Wins Three-Year Contract Extension Award for Queensland Coal Mine
data.description:
Macmahon Holdings secured a three-year extension for its contract at the Byerwen coking coal mine in Queensland, according to a Thursday filing with the Australian bourse.

The extension, which starts in October, will see Macmahon continue to provide open-cut mining services, including load and haul and crusher feed operations at the site, the filing said.

The contract extension is expected to contribute about AU$900 million to Macmahon's secured order book, the filing added.

The extension is contingent on finalizing certain documentation by Sept. 30, the company said.

data.title: 30-Year Bond Auction Set to Test Demand After Trump Pauses Tariffs
data.description:
Karishma Vanjani

Investors want assurance that demand for U.S. Treasuries is still alive and kicking, and a solid auction Thursday for the 30-year bond could help alleviate their doubts.

The Treasury Department is getting ready to sell $22 billion worth of bonds expiring in about 30 years. The auction comes at a time when U.S. bonds, the world's safest asset, have been getting dumped by investors.

The 30-year yield, at 4.786%, this week marked its largest three-day gain since the pandemic. It rose 0.072 percentage point on Wednesday, after taking a small breather when President Donald Trump announced a pause on tariffs. Higher yields mean lower prices and demand.

Despite the tariff pause, it is easy to see reasons why a selloff could continue. Bond traders, betting on the 10- and 30-year Treasury, typically take a longer-term view of the U.S. economy. They're currently assuming inflation is here to stay, boosted by current trade policies, tighter immigration, and the effects of large fiscal spending.

There has also been speculation from Wall Street that foreign buyers have been shunning Treasuries as a way to punish U.S. trade policies, in a "sell America trade." There is no concrete data to verify the claim. Foreign buyers have bought about 9% of the two- to 30-year dated Treasuries that were issued last month, nearly in line with the levels seen in the prior two months of the year.

The auction of 10-year bonds on Wednesday showed solid demand, calming market fears. It also showed "indirect buyers" — a category that typically includes foreign central banks and private investors — bought a record 87.9% of the supply allotted to them, above the average of 70%.

Foreign buyers can also participate in auctions directly, meaning the auction numbers can't definitively indicate if they are fleeing.

The auction of 30-year bonds may help provide some clarity on overall demand. If investor interest is strong overall, it can pull yields lower, indicating the market is confident about the bond's safe haven status in what looks to be an on-again, off-again tariff world.

If interest isn't strong, it will suggest the other problems were too big to ignore.

Write to Karishma Vanjani at karishma.vanjani@dowjones.com.

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: a2 Milk Target Price Raised 8.3% to A$7.85/Share by Bell Potter>A2M.AU
Oil Prices Continue Rebound in Late Trading
data.description:
By Ryan Dezember

Oil prices continued to climb in late trading Wednesday, with benchmark U.S. crude futures rising to about $63 a barrel.

  • That's up from Tuesday's settled price of $59.58, which was the lowest in four years.
  • But it's still problematically low for many U.S. drillers. Oil-and-gas executives surveyed last month by the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas said that on average they needed prices to be around $65 to profitably drill a new well. Consulting firm Rystad Energy estimated that domestic producers need $62 oil to break even, including paying their debt and dividends.
  • Despite President Trump pausing many of the tariffs he levied on many countries last week, he escalated his trade war with China, which puts global growth-and oil demand-at risk. Banks have been cutting their oil-price forecasts. Goldman Sachs, which accounted for a large reduction in Trump's tariffs in its outlook, over the weekend lowered its forecast for benchmark U.S. crude futures at year end to $58, from $62.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: JB Hi-Fi Raised to Buy From Hold by Bell Potter >JBH.AU
Deep Yellow Target Price Cut 12% to A$1.45/Share by Bell Potter>DYL.AU
Westpac to Open Three New Banking Service Centers in Regional Australia
data.description:
Westpac Banking will open three new banking service centers in New South Wales, Victoria, and Tasmania as part of its investment in regional Australia, according to a Thursday statement.

The service centers are in Moree in New South Wales, which will open later this year; and Leongatha in Victoria and Smithton in Tasmania, which will both open in early 2026, the statement said.

Services include face-to-face support for retail and business customers, as well as digital banking assistance and access to cash, per the statement.

Westpac said work is underway to roll out its services to additional regional locations.

The bank plans to add 150 new business bankers across its regional network over the next three years, according to the statement.

Westpac's shares were up over 1% in New Zealand.

data.title: Regal Partners Target Price Cut 33% to A$3.35/Share by Bell Potter>RPL.AU
ZOZO to Acquire UK's Lyst in $154 Million Deal
data.description:
ZOZO will acquire UK-based shopping platform Lyst for $154 million through a newly established British subsidiary, according to a Wednesday filing on the Tokyo Stock Exchange.

The move marks a shift in ZOZO's global strategy, moving from licensing and partnerships to direct overseas operations.

The company said Lyst's AI-driven, inventory-free model and reach across 27,000 brands align with its growth ambitions in the US and Europe.

Lyst reported 50 million pounds sterling in net sales and 400,000 pounds in operating profit for the year ended March 2024.

ZOZO aims to integrate its sizing and fit technology with Lyst's recommendation engine to broaden global reach and enhance product discovery.

The deal, expected to close by April 30, includes all outstanding shares and values Lyst at about 23.1 billion yen. ZOZO estimates another 1.2 billion yen in advisory and transaction costs.

data.title: Quantum Cats NFT floor price plunges 54% post-Taproot Wizards mint
data.description:
The floor price of Bitcoin NFT Quantum Cats halved in value last week, falling from 0.087 BTC on March 30 to 0.04 BTC as of Saturday, April 5. Meanwhile, the floor prices of other notable Bitcoin NFT collections remained relatively flat in comparison. For example, NodeMonkes and Bitcoin Puppets declined by just 5% and 8%, respectively, over the same period.

The significant and isolated ~54% decline in Quantum Cats’ floor price is linked to the recent Taproot Wizards mint that began on March 25.

Quantum Cats holders were given a discount for the Taproot Wizards mint, thus incentivizing people to buy and hold them prior to the mint. Specifically, collectors who owned pairs of "entangled" Quantum Cats were given a 50% discount on the mint price, from 0.2 BTC to 0.1 BTC. However, after the Taproot Wizards mint, those who bought and held Quantum Cats solely to access the discount no longer had the same incentive to keep the NFTs, thus prompting them to sell in a classic example of a "sell the news/event."

This event has now brought Quantum Cats' floor price closer to its peers, such as NodeMonkes and Bitcoin Puppets, as opposed to the relatively significant premium it commanded from late December 2024 due to anticipation and hype of the aforementioned Taproot Wizards mint.

This is an excerpt from The Block's Data & Insights newsletter. Dig into the numbers making up the industry's most thought-provoking trends.

Disclaimer: The Block is an independent media outlet that delivers news, research, and data. As of November 2023, Foresight Ventures is a majority investor of The Block. Foresight Ventures invests in other companies in the crypto space. Crypto exchange Bitget is an anchor LP for Foresight Ventures. The Block continues to operate independently to deliver objective, impactful, and timely information about the crypto industry. Here are our current financial disclosures.

data.title: Energy Transfer signs deal with MidOcean for Lake Charles, Louisiana, LNG project
data.description:
U.S. pipeline operator Energy Transfer said on Wednesday it entered into a nonbinding agreement with MidOcean Energy to jointly develop the Lake Charles LNG export facility in Louisiana.

MidOcean will pay for 30% of the construction costs of the facility and receive 30% of the LNG production, or roughly 5.0 million metric tonnes per annum (MTPA). Energy Transfer is the developer and operator of the plant.

The agreement is nonbinding because it depends on Energy Transfer making the final investment decision to go ahead with the plant.

Energy Transfer now has final sales agreements and nonbinding sales agreements for 16 million metric tonnes per annum of the 16.5 million MTPA capacity for the plant.

The agreement should move Energy Transfer closer to making the final decision on the plant.

LNG developers typically use sales and purchase agreements when they make their case to banks to borrow the money they need to develop production facilities.

Lake Charles was the first LNG project impacted by the Biden administration's refusal to grant an extension to Energy Transfer's license to export to countries other than those that have free trade agreements with the United States. Former President Joe Biden subsequently declared a moratorium on new export licenses for LNG plants pending an environmental impact study.

Non-FTA licenses are important for LNG project developments because they allow U.S. producers to sell LNG to most importing countries, and not just those that have free trade agreements with the U.S.

The administration of President Donald Trump has issued several non-FTA licenses since it lifted Biden's moratorium, but has yet to grant an extension or a new license to Energy Transfer.

Under the agreement, MidOcean Energy will have the option to arrange for its own gas supply for its share of LNG production from the plant, and will commit to long-term gas transportation on Energy Transfer pipelines, the companies said.

data.title: Maravai LifeSciences Holdings, Inc. (MRVI) Admits to Incorrect Revenue Recognition, Ineffective Internal Controls; Class Action Pending – Hagens Berman
data.description:
SAN FRANCISCO, April 09, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — On Mar. 19, 2025, the price of Maravai LifeSciences (NASDAQ: MRVI) shares fell almost 6% after the company warned investors against relying on previously filed financial statements. This move lower follows a 21% drop on Feb. 25, 2025 after the company postponed its Q4 and FY 2024 earnings release and investor conference call.

A class action lawsuit has been filed against Maravai, a global provider of life science reagents and services to researchers and biotech innovators. The suit, captioned Nelson v. Maravai Lifesciences Holdings, Inc., et al., No. 3:25-cv-00499-AGS-AHG (S.D. Cal.), represents investors who purchased Maravai securities between August 7, 2024 and February 24, 2025.

Hagens Berman is investigating the alleged claims and urges Maravai investors who suffered substantial losses to submit your losses now.

Class Period: Aug. 7, 2024 – Feb. 24, 2025

Lead Plaintiff Deadline: May 5, 2025

Visit: www.hbsslaw.com/investor-fraud/mrvi

Contact the Firm Now: MRVI@hbsslaw.com | 844-916-0895

Recent Development:

In a recent development, on Mar. 18, 2025, Maravai advised investors against relying on its Q2 and Q3 2024 financial reports, which the company said would be restated to correct the improper revenue recognition. The company also advised investors that its “disclosure controls and procedures were not effective[]” and its “internal control over financial reporting was ineffective[.]”

This news sent the price of Maravai shares down almost 6% the next day.

Maravai LifeSciences Holdings, Inc. (MRVI) Securities Class Action:

The litigation is focused on the propriety of Maravai’s assurances that its financial statements are prepared in accordance with applicable accounting rules and that its internal controls over financial reporting are sufficient.

The complaint alleges that Maravai made false and misleading statements while failing to disclose crucial information. Specifically, the suit contends that the company failed to disclose to investors that:

  • It lacked adequate internal controls over financial reporting related to revenue recognition;
  • As a result, it inaccurately recognized revenue on certain transactions during fiscal 2024; and
  • Its goodwill was overstated.

The company’s assurances came into question on Feb. 25, 2025, when Maravai announced that it postponed its Q4 and FY 2024 earnings release and conference call scheduled for that day.

Maravai revealed that it:

  • is assessing a goodwill impairment charge associated with a previous acquisition;
  • overstated Q2 2024 revenue by approximately $3.9 million as a result of its recording the amount upon shipment in the final week of the quarter rather than on receipt by the customer; and
  • is assessing the sufficiency of its internal controls over financial reporting, including internal controls over revenue recognition.

This news sent the price of Maravai shares down over 21% on Feb. 25, 2025, with one analyst (Baird) slashing its rating from outperform to neutral and its price target from $9 to $3.

“We are investigating whether Maravai may have intentionally recorded unearned revenues,” said Reed Kathrein, the Hagens Berman Partner leading the firm's probe.

If you invested in Maravai and have substantial losses, or have knowledge that may assist the firm’s investigation, submit your losses now.

Whistleblowers: Persons with non-public information regarding Maravai should consider their options to help in the investigation or take advantage of the SEC Whistleblower program. Under the new program, whistleblowers who provide original information may receive rewards totaling up to 30 percent of any successful recovery made by the SEC. For more information, call Reed Kathrein at 844-916-0895 or email MRVI@hbsslaw.com.

About Hagens Berman

Hagens Berman is a global plaintiffs’ rights complex litigation firm focusing on corporate accountability. The firm is home to a robust practice and represents investors as well as whistleblowers, workers, consumers and others in cases achieving real results for those harmed by corporate negligence and other wrongdoings. Hagens Berman’s team has secured more than $2.9 billion in this area of law. More about the firm and its successes can be found at hbsslaw.com. Follow the firm for updates and news at @ClassActionLaw.

Contact:

Reed Kathrein, 844-916-0895

data.title: On-Again Off-Again Tariffs Send Americans Rushing to Stock Up
data.description:
By Natasha Khan and Rachel Louise Ensign

Flashbacks of shortages and price hikes during the Covid pandemic still haunted Brandy Galloway, so when she heard about the new tariffs last week she decided it was time to take action. Galloway and her husband bought rice, extra virgin olive oil, tomato sauce, 7 pounds of coffee and every type of bean they could find.

"We would have bought triple if we could afford it," said Galloway, who is 37 and runs a baked-goods business from her home in Longmont, Colo. Everything was expensive but, she said, "in a few weeks, it's going to be even worse."

Then on Wednesday, President Trump announced a 90-day pause on certain tariffs to most countries. The sudden U-turn reordered markets and economic forecasts, and shocked consumers and businesses.

The uncertainty feels all too familiar. Americans stung by the Covid-era experience of shortages, inflation and supply-chain shock are determined not to get caught on the wrong side of Trump's tariffs. That is prompting some of them to stock up on everyday goods and accelerate big purchases like cars and appliances. Consumers who are afraid of the direction the country is headed in are especially eager to make these moves.

Galloway suspects that even if Trump's tariffs don't cause prices to spike, retailers will use them as an excuse to increase prices anyway. Sharp inflation sparked by the pandemic pushed her into debt just for monthly groceries and expenses. She said late Wednesday that she had no reason to trust that Trump's pause would change much — especially since the president said that tariffs on China would rise to 125%.

Data from Earnest Analytics, which tracked anonymous card data of millions of Americans, showed consumer spending rose 33% at Apple, 26% at Restoration Hardware and 10% at Home Depot this past Saturday compared with the average of the previous four Saturdays.

Not all stores are getting a boost. Walmart, the country's largest retailer by annual revenue, hasn't seen panic buying, Chief Financial Officer John David Rainey said Wednesday.

Companies themselves also are stockpiling to get ahead of tariffs, a tricky balancing act where they could get stuck with extra inventory. They're also advertising tariff costs and urging consumers to buy now before they raise prices.

Ford Motor is extending its employee discounts to the wider buying public. Furniture store Raymour & Flanigan is telling customers to "lock in pre-tariff pricing." Jewelry chain Ana Luisa told customers, "If you've had your eye on something, now is the time to shop."

At Eagle Tire Pros auto-repair shop in La Follette, Tenn., a dozen customers have come in to change tires early to get ahead of higher prices, owner Brandon Johnson said. He rushed to double the usual inventory of many of his tires after the April 2 announcement.

He said Wednesday he was still glad he'd stocked up — and thankful that many of the tariffs had been paused: "It is going to give us time to breathe and assess the environment."

The lingering memory of the pandemic is likely a factor in the motivation to stockpile, said Yuriy Gorodnichenko, an economics professor at the University of California, Berkeley.

"People remember this stuff. If they know how to store stuff, they will do it again," he said.

In a survey from Gorodnichenko and two other professors in December and January, 43% of respondents said they would be more likely to purchase and stockpile goods in anticipation of new tariffs. Some 36% said the uncertainty would make them more likely to set aside money.

Rising prices have dominated Americans' complaints and were a big factor in the 2024 presidential election. Spurred by pandemic buying and snarled supply chains, consumer prices rose more than 20% over the past five years.

Linda Leeson, who lives in the Cleveland area, changed her budget after Trump won. She has purchased a new car, a washer and dryer and lumber for a rural rental property she owns.

During the pandemic, Leeson struggled to find lumber, and when she did it was very expensive. When her son needed a new car, only vehicles with manual transmissions were available.

Two weeks ago, she spent about $60 on a year's supply of a French vinegar that she uses on salads and vegetables. "I figure a year from now this will be over or the world is in such chaos that I won't care about vinegar," the 71-year-old retired technology executive said.

Wednesday's announcement hasn't changed her mindset. "I'm very glad it happened but I don't believe this is anything other than a pause," she said.

Fear of rising prices will likely boost sales of certain items in the short term, said Don Rissmiller, chief economist at brokerage firm Strategas. People will likely buy durable products they would have eventually bought anyway like phones and cars, he said.

"If you were thinking about a car purchase two months from now and you have the means and financing, there's a strong incentive to do that," he said. Some automakers posted big increases in sales in March.

Not everyone is in a position to stockpile. Many Americans are feeling financially stretched after the recent run of high inflation and don't have extra money to put toward big purchases. That's different from the Covid era, when people were flush with government stimulus money and other savings after being stuck at home.

By the third quarter last year, working-class and middle-class households were cutting spending. Inflation-adjusted consumer spending in February edged up just 0.1% from the prior month, according to the Commerce Department, and it fell in January.

Some are trying to save more because they are worried about a big downturn, or even a recession. Even those who accelerated purchases are sometimes cutting back in other areas.

Mélisse Brunet started to worry about rising prices after she saw Trump's flurry of executive orders at the start of his term. "It just appeared very clearly that things would be changed in a very big way," said Brunet, a 48-year-old orchestra conductor who lives in Philadelphia.

Her dishwasher broke and she had her eye on a replacement from ASKO where the door pops open when the washing cycle is finished to help the items dry.

In early February, she spent $10,000 on the dishwasher, a refrigerator, and a washer and dryer. She is saving her old appliances because she thinks their resale value will rise.

Brunet said she has stopped buying from Amazon.com and is getting clothing and furniture secondhand. She's looking into growing some of her own vegetables.

Alex Tabarrok, a professor of economics at George Mason University, said that even if all the tariffs disappear tomorrow, so much chaos and uncertainty has been created that costs are going to be higher for some time.

"Consumers will have to weigh buying ahead with saving a bit extra in fear of a recession," he added.

Tabarrok chose buying ahead. He and his wife recently scooped up a $799 marble-and-bronze coffee table from West Elm. They had been eyeing it for a while, and given that it's made in India, they went ahead and bought it. They also stocked up on treats for their dog, Ella, and wine.

He's still happy with his decision. "We don't know what will happen tomorrow, let alone 90 days from now," Tabarrok said.

Besides, he likes the table.

Write to Natasha Khan at natasha.khan@wsj.com and Rachel Louise Ensign at Rachel.Ensign@wsj.com.

data.title: Amplitude Energy Target Price Cut 11% to A$0.25/Share by Macquarie>AEL.AU
Accounting Firms Baker Tilly and Moss Adams Near Merger, Sources Say
Accounting Firms Baker Tilly and Moss Adams Near Merger
data.description:
By Mark Maurer

Accounting firm Moss Adams is in advanced talks to receive private-equity investment through a merger with peer Baker Tilly, people familiar with the matter said.

A deal, which could be announced in the coming weeks, would give the combined accounting and consulting firms a total of more than $3 billion in annual revenue, based on 2024 revenue. That would make it the sixth-largest accounting firm in the U.S., ahead of BDO, CBIZ and Grant Thornton.

The combined firm would be led by senior leadership from both organizations, one of the people said.

Baker Tilly last year sold a stake to Hellman & Friedman and Valeas Capital Partners. It is the second-largest accounting firm to sell a stake to private-equity investors, behind Grant Thornton.

Baker Tilly said it has been transparent about its strategy to grow through strategic mergers. "We are continually exploring opportunities with respected firms that align with our growth expectations and will enhance our ability to serve the middle market," the firm said. Moss Adams said it doesn't comment on market speculation.

A deal hasn't been finalized, and it is still possible talks could fall apart or another suitor could prevail.

The potential deal would give Baker Tilly a greater presence on the U.S. West Coast and Moss Adams more international operations.

In 2024, Moss Adams had 73 Securities and Exchange Commission-registered audit clients, including special-purpose acquisition companies, and Baker Tilly had 38, according to Ideagen Audit Analytics.

Chicago-based Baker Tilly reported U.S. revenue of more than $1.8 billion in 2024, the most recent year available. That ranks it 11th among U.S. accounting firms, just ahead of Seattle-based Moss Adams at 12th, with about $1.3 billion, according to Accounting Today.

Baker Tilly is aiming to increase its U.S. revenue by up to three times by 2030, Jere Shawver, CEO of Baker Tilly's U.S. audit arm, said last fall.

CohnReznick and EisnerAmper are also among those that have taken private-equity money since 2021. Most accounting firms that have sold a stake to private equity have done so without a merger with a peer. They are motivated by the capital boost to help address partner retirements, costly technology investments and an accountant shortage straining their resources.

Write to Mark Maurer at mark.maurer@wsj.com

data.title: Investor Alert: Class Action Lawsuits Against General Motors, DocGo, Estée Lauder, and Axsome Therapeutics Survive Motions to Dismiss — Johnson Fistel, PLLP Encourages Shareholders to Contact the Firm
data.description:
SAN DIEGO, April 09, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — Johnson Fistel, PLLP is investigating potential violations of federal and state laws by certain officers of the following companies: General Motors Company , DocGo Inc. , The Estée Lauder Companies Inc. , and Axsome Therapeutics, Inc. .

General Motors Company

Johnson Fistel, PLLP is investigating claims on behalf of General Motors Company against certain officers and directors.

If you are a long-term shareholder of General Motors, continuously holding your shares since February 24, 2021, you may have standing to hold the company harmless from the alleged harm caused by the Company's officers and directors by making them personally responsible. You may also be able to assist in reforming the Company's corporate governance to prevent future wrongdoing. You can click or copy and paste the link below in a browser to join this action: https://www.johnsonfistel.com/investigations/general-motors-company-3

Recently, the securities class action survived a motion to dismiss. According to the Complaint, the General Motors Company is an automotive manufacturing company that designs, builds, and sells trucks, crossovers, cars, and automobile parts worldwide. The Company markets its vehicles primarily under the Buick, Cadillac, Chevrolet, GMC, Baojun, and Wuling brand names.

The Complaint alleges that throughout the Class Period, Defendants made materially false and misleading statements regarding the Company's business, operations, and prospects. Specifically, the Complaint alleges Defendants made false and/or misleading statements and/or failed to disclose that: (i) GM downplayed concerns with its vehicles' airbags and the need to record additional warranty accruals for related product recalls; (ii) GM overstated the extent and efficacy of its efforts to analyze defects in its vehicles' airbag inflators; (iii) Cruise's AVs and/or AV technology were less safe and well-developed than Defendants had led investors, regulators, and the general public to believe; (iv) accordingly, regulatory approval of Cruise's AV products was unsustainable and the prospects for widespread regulatory approval and adoption of Cruise's AV products were overstated; (v) all the foregoing subjected GM to an increased risk of governmental and/or regulatory scrutiny and enforcement action, significant legal liabilities, product recalls, and reputational harm; and (vi) as a result, Defendants' public statements were materially false and/or misleading at all relevant times.

DocGo Inc.

Johnson Fistel, PLLP is investigating claims on behalf of DocGo Inc. against certain of its officers and directors. Recently, the securities class action complaint alleging false or misleading statements and/or omitted material adverse information survived in part the motion to dismiss.

If you are a current, long-term shareholder of the company, continuously holding your shares since November 5, 2021, you may have standing to hold the company harmless from the alleged harm caused by the Company's officers and directors by making them personally responsible. You may also be able to assist in reforming the Company's corporate governance to prevent future wrongdoing. You can click or copy and paste the link below in a browser to join this action: https://www.johnsonfistel.com/investigations/docgo-inc-2

Recently, the Court denied the defendants’ motion to dismiss a shareholder class action complaint. The Complaint alleges that, throughout the Class Period, Defendants made materially false and misleading statements regarding the Company’s business, operations, and compliance policies. Specifically, the Complaint alleges Defendants made false and/or misleading statements and/or failed to disclose that: (i) DocGo’s executive hiring processes were inadequate to fully review and vet the professional and academic backgrounds of job candidates; (ii) the foregoing increased the likelihood of disruptive executive turnover; (iii) contrary to its representations to investors, DocGo had overstated the efficacy of its mobile health and medical transportation services, the very services contemplated by the Relocation Contract; (iv) all of the foregoing, once revealed, was likely to subject DocGo to significant reputational and/or regulatory scrutiny that would negatively impact the Company’s financial position and/or prospects; and (v) as a result, the Company’s public statements were materially false and misleading at all relevant times.

The Estée Lauder Companies Inc.

Johnson Fistel, PLLP is investigating claims on behalf of The Estée Lauder Companies Inc. against certain of its officers and directors.

If you are a current, long-term shareholder of Estée Lauder, continuously holding shares since February 3, 2022, you may have standing to hold the company harmless from the alleged harm caused by the Company's officers and directors by making them personally responsible. You may also be able to assist in reforming the Company's corporate governance to prevent future wrongdoing. You can click or copy and paste the link below in a browser to join this action: https://www.johnsonfistel.com/investigations/the-estee-lauder-companies-inc

Recently, the shareholder class action lawsuit against the company survived the Defendants’ motion to dismiss. The Complaint alleges that during the Class Period, Defendants misled investors with unrealistic and materially false statements about market demand Estee's products and its inventory levels and that these statements concealed the truth about Estee's weakness in the market until, on May 3, 2023, Estee announced weaker sales and profit for the year than estimated and accordingly cut its fiscal year outlook for a third consecutive time. The price of Estee stock declined from $245.22 per share on May 2, 2023 to $202.70 per share on May 3, 2023.

Axsome Therapeutics, Inc.

Johnson Fistel, PLLP is investigating claims on behalf of Axsome Therapeutics, Inc. against certain of its officers and directors.

If you are a current, long-term shareholder of Axsome, continuously holding shares since May 10, 2021, you may have standing to hold the company harmless from the alleged harm caused by the Company's officers and directors by making them personally responsible. You may also be able to assist in reforming the Company's corporate governance to prevent future wrongdoing. You can click or copy and paste the link below in a browser to join this action: https://www.johnsonfistel.com/investigations/axsome-therapeutics-inc

Recently, the shareholder class action lawsuit against the company survived the defendants' motion to dismiss. The class action complaint was filed against the company. The Complaint alleges that throughout the class period, Defendants made false and/or misleading statements, as well as failed to disclose material adverse facts about the Company’s business, operations, and prospects. Specifically, the Complaint alleges Defendants made false and/or misleading statements and/or failed to disclose that: (i) Axsome’s chemistry, manufacturing, and control ("CMC") practices were deficient with respect to AXS-07 and its manufacturing process; (ii) as a result, Axsome was unlikely to submit the AXS-07 NDA on its initially represented timeline; (iii) the foregoing CMC issues remained unresolved at the time that the FDA reviewed the AXS-07 NDA; (iv) accordingly, the FDA was unlikely to approve the AXS-07 NDA; (v) as a result of all the foregoing, Axsome had overstated AXS-07’s regulatory and commercial prospects; and (vi) as a result, the Company’s public statements were materially false and misleading at all relevant times.

About Johnson Fistel, PLLP | Top Law Firm, Securities Fraud, Investors Rights:

Johnson Fistel, PLLP is a nationally recognized shareholder rights law firm with offices in California, New York, Georgia, and Colorado. The firm represents individual and institutional investors in shareholder derivative and securities class action lawsuits. We also extend our services to foreign investors who have purchased on US exchanges. Stay updated with news on stock drops and learn how Johnson Fistel, PLLP can help you recover your losses. For more information about the firm and its attorneys, please visit http://www.johnsonfistel.com.

Achievements: In 2024, Johnson Fistel was honored to be ranked in the Top 10 Plaintiff Law Firms by the ISS Securities Class Action Services. This recognition underscores our effectiveness in advocating for investors, having recovered approximately $90,725,000 for aggrieved clients in cases where we served as lead or co-lead counsel. This notable accomplishment marks the eighth occasion our firm has been recognized as a top plaintiffs’ securities law firm in the United States, as determined by the total dollar value of final recoveries.
